design_philosophy.core_loop: Pilot attack helicopter through vertically scrolling stages, destroy military vehicles and bosses, manage limited ammunition (bombs) and power-ups while avoiding collision with enemy fire
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (bombs/firepower) vs. enemy density; constant forward momentum vs. player agency to pause/position; aggressive offense (bomb usage) vs. defensive dodging
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Commanding an elite military attack helicopter, unleashing devastating area-denial weapons against overwhelming odds
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Design philosophy explicitly prioritizes aggressive, stress-relieving gameplay through bombs as offensive tools rather than defensive safety nets. Developers intentionally made enemies tougher to maintain balance around bomb availability. Progression based on loops (second loop increases difficulty) rather than escalating stage design alone. 'Kyūkyoku' (Ultimate) positioning signals pursuit of transcendent shooting experience.

difficulty.lives_system: Players start with two lives; bonus lives awarded at point thresholds and thereafter at regular intervals
difficulty.continues_system: Arcade continue system (insert more credits); NES version behavior unclear from sources
difficulty.checkpointing: Regional variant: Japanese version uses respawn system where ship reappears at death location; US version uses checkpoint system where player restarts at last checkpoint reached before death
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Power-up collection (weapons switching via color icons, firepower 'S' icons, bombs via 'B' icons) provides tactical loadout choices. Bomb deployment is explicitly aggressive decision-making, not reactive. Firepower resets on hit, creating risk/reward tension around engagement depth.

progression.upgrade_system: Mid-run weapon switching (red/blue/green/yellow icons yield different weapon types); firepower increments via 'S' icons; bomb stock increases via 'B' icons. All upgrades are temporary and reset on damage.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Ten increasingly difficult linear stages, each terminated by mandatory boss encounter. Second loop available after stage 10, with increased enemy density and bullet patterns.
replayability_hooks: Score chasing with gold medal collection (3000-point bonuses)
Weapon selection mastery (four weapon types to optimize for different stage layouts)
High score table with regional password system (Japanese version: 'KTTAISONMAI89PRTN23Y')
Regional differences create variant experiences (respawn vs. checkpoint, ending text, staff rolls)

world_building_techniques: Military theme (tanks, battleships, artillery as enemy types)
Helipad landing zones as level conclusion markers
Progressive scenery (constantly scrolling background until helipad reached)
Enemy carriers and weapon pickups suggest military supply chains

boss_design.philosophy: Mandatory finale for each of ten stages; designed to force engagement with accumulated player skill (firepower, bomb management, weapon selection). Boss encounters conclude scrolling sequences and anchor progression gates.

faction_indicators: Enemy military vehicles and installations suggest geopolitical conflict
danger_signals: Collision-based damage (touhou-style bullet patterns, not explicitly mentioned but standard to genre)
Dense bullet patterns in loop 2 indicating escalating threat
environmental_storytelling: Scrolling military landscape (tanks, battleships, artillery positions)
Helipad landing zones mark safe havens and stage conclusions
Enemy carrier destruction as gameplay event tied to weapon acquisition

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor) - Twin Cobra NES documentation: regional differences, unused text, checkpoint/respawn mechanics

wikipedia - Twin Cobra entry: core gameplay loop, bomb/weapon system design philosophy, developer interviews (Masahiro Yuge, Tatsuya Uemura), progression structure (10 stages + loops), lives/continue system, power-up mechanics
